coredata.dc:title: Controlled preparation of MoS2/PbBiO2I hybrid microspheres with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic behaviour
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ming
Yin, Sheng
Wu, Ting
Di, Jun
Ji, Mengxia
Wang, Bin
Chen, Yong
Xia, Jiexiang
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description:
Novel MoS2/PbBiO2I hybrid microspheres were controllably synthesized via the ionic liquid 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Hmim]I) assisted solvothermal method. The morphologies, structures, optical and electronic properties of the materials were explored by XRD, XPS, SEM, TEM, DRS, EIS and photocurrent analysis. The photocatalytic activity of MoS2/PbBiO2I was evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB), antibiotic agent ciprofloxacin (CIP) and bisphenol A (BPA) under visible light irradiation. The photodegradation results showed that the activity of MoS2/PbBiO2I was higher than that of pure PbBiO2I because of suitable band alignment, a larger specific surface area, an enhanced light absorption region, and stronger photocurrent intensity arising from the interface interaction between MoS2 and PbBiO2I. The 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I showed the best photocatalytic performance. Electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments and free radical trapping experiments showed that the superoxide radicals (O2
−) and holes (h+) were determined to be the main active species for the photocatalysis process. A possible mechanism was presented based on the detection and analysis results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2
PbBiO2I
Photocatalysis
Microspheres
Visible-light irradiation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical and electronic properties of MoS2/PbBiO2I photocatalysts

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Support Program of Zhenjiang
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21476098 , 21576123 and 21676128 ) and the Science and Technology Support Program of Zhenjiang ( SH2014018 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for pure PbBiO2I and MoS2/PbBiO2I materials with different MoS2 contents.
XPS spectrum of 5 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I sample: (a) survey of the sample; (b) Pb 4f; (c) Bi 4f; (d) O 1s; (e) I 3d; (f) Mo 3d; (g) S 2p.
(a–c) SEM images of the PbBiO2I、MoS2 and 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I materials: (a) PbBiO2I; (b) MoS2; (c) 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I; (d) EDS of the 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I materials.
Nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherms of (a) pure PbBiO2I and 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I materials; (b) Pore size distribution of 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I material.
The diffuse reflection spectra of pure PbBiO2I and MoS2/PbBiO2I materials with different MoS2 contents and (α Ephoton)1/2 vs Ephoton curves of the as-prepared PbBiO2I sample (inner).
Transient photocurrent responses of 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I sample and pure PbBiO2I sample.

(a) Different amount of composite MoS2/PbBiO2I materials on RhB degradation efficiency; (b) Temporal UV–vis absorption spectral changes of CIP by 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I; (c) 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I and PbBiO2I materials on CIP degradation efficiency; (d) 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I and PbBiO2I materials on BPA degradation efficiency.

DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra recorded with 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I materials in (a) methanol dispersion (for DMPO
O2 −) and (b) aqueous dispersion (for DMPO-
OH), under visible light irradiation.

DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra recorded with PbBiO2I materials in (a) methanol dispersion (for DMPO
O2 −) and (b) aqueous dispersion (for DMPO-
OH), under visible light irradiation.
Photocatalysis process of pollution over MoS2/PbBiO2I under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Controlled preparation of MoS2/PbBiO2I hybrid microspheres with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic behaviour

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Novel MoS2/PbBiO2I hybrid microspheres were controllably synthesized via the ionic liquid 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Hmim]I) assisted solvothermal method. The morphologies, structures, optical and electronic properties of the materials were explored by XRD, XPS, SEM, TEM, DRS, EIS and photocurrent analysis. The photocatalytic activity of MoS2/PbBiO2I was evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB), antibiotic agent ciprofloxacin (CIP) and bisphenol A (BPA) under visible light irradiation. The photodegradation results showed that the activity of MoS2/PbBiO2I was higher than that of pure PbBiO2I because of suitable band alignment, a larger specific surface area, an enhanced light absorption region, and stronger photocurrent intensity arising from the interface interaction between MoS2 and PbBiO2I. The 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I showed the best photocatalytic performance. Electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments and free radical trapping experiments showed that the superoxide radicals (O2
−) and holes (h+) were determined to be the main active species for the photocatalysis process. A possible mechanism was presented based on the detection and analysis results.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis has attracted much attention as a green advanced oxidation technique used for environmental remediation and energy conversion, including photodegradation of organic dyes in wastewater, hydrogen production from water splitting, and CO2 conversion to renewable fuels, because of its low cost, simple operation and absence of secondary pollution [1–5]. Traditional photocatalysts such as TiO2 suffer from wide bandgaps (3.2 eV for anatase) and only respond to UV light that accounts for less than 5% of sunlight energy [6–8]. Exploitation of new methods that enable larger utilization of sunlight to achieve a higher quantum efficiency has become a promising research topic. For this reason, much effort has been expended, with some researchers modifying the TiO2 semiconductor material [9–11] and some exploring non-TiO2 semiconductor materials such as metal oxides [12,13], composite metal oxides [14], nitrides [15] and bismuth-based materials [16]. In efforts to exploit novel photocatalyst systems working under visible light, it has been revealed that orbitals of some p-block metals with a d10 configuration [17–20], such as Ag 4d, Sn 5s and Bi 6s, could hybridize with O 2p levels to form a new preferable hybridized valence band (VB), thus narrowing the band gap to harvest more visible light [21]. Currently, due to the low toxicity and rich reserves of Bi, many bismuth-based materials such as Bi2O3 [22], CaBi2O4 [23], Bi2WO6 [24,25], BiVO4 [26], Bi2O2CO3 [27] and BiOIO3 [28], have been employed in the photocatalytic processes.

Among these, bismuth oxyhalides are of immense importance for their outstanding optical and electrical properties, suitable band-structure, as well as high chemical stability, nontoxicity, low cost, and immunity to corrosion [21,29]. All bismuth oxyhalides compounds are crystallized in a tetragonal matlockite (PbFCl-type) structure made of many [BilOmXn] monolayers, with each monolayer consisting of [BilOm] and [Xn] layers with [BilOm] layer in the middle wrapped by two [Xn] layers interacted by the nonbonding (van der Waals) forces [30–32]. The bismuth-based metal oxyhalides are those in which the other metal elements A are introduced into the bismuth halide lattice and replace some Bi elements in the [Bi2O2]2+ layer to form a covalent metal oxide ([ABiOy]+) layer. The layer of [ABiOy]+ and the halogen ion alternately arranged to form the ABiOyX (X = Cl, Br, I) material [33]. Due to the introduction of different metal elements, the composite metal bismuth halide may possess both a bandgap enabling visible light response and higher mobility for the current carrier and is considered to be a promising photocatalytic material [34,35]. To date, SrBiO2Br [36], BaBiO2Br [37], CaBiO2Cl [38], CdBiO2Cl [39] and other materials have been prepared via different methods.

Lead (Pb) is the fourth main element of the p-block in the periodic table. With its 6s2 6p2 outer electron structure, the most stable form of lead (Pb2+) is formed by losing the two outer electrons. In addition, Bi and Pb are adjacent in the periodic table, and their atomic number is nearly same, thus the difference between the ionic radii of Pb2+ and Bi3+ is very small. Thus, when Pb2+ is used to replace Bi3+ in BiOCl, the crystal structure of bismuth oxychloride does not change greatly and the structure is still stable, so bismuth oxychloride lead has become the focus of research because of its small bandgap, better visible light response ability, and internal electric field (IEF) that favours the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Shan et al. [40] prepared PbBiO2Cl powders using the solid phase method, showing good photocatalytic activity and good cyclic stability in the process of methyl orange (MO) degradation under visible and UV light. Yu et al. [41] prepared bipyramid-like PbBiO2Cl photocatalyst exposing {0 0 2} and {1 0 3} planes by a simple hydrothermal method. The results demonstrated that PbBiO2Cl exhibited better photocatalytic activity compared to TiO2, N-TiO2 and C3N4. Xiao et al. [42] synthesized two dimensional (2D) PbBiO2Br nanosheets by a solvothermal method, and the results showed that ultrathin PbBiO2Br nanosheets displayed enhanced photocatalytic performance compared to their bulk counterpart in the degradation of methyl orange (MO). However, there have been few studies on PbBiO2I materials, let alone on PbBiO2I-based composite photocatalysts.

Molybdenum disulphide (MoS2) is a widely known layered transition-metal dichalcogenide that is extensively used because of its unique lamellar structure, high surface activity and large specific surface area. Many studies on the photocatalysis and photoelectrocatalytic activities have proved that MoS2 could be a promising candidate for use in hydrodesulphurization [43,44], hydrogen evolution reactions [45–47], and biosensors [48]. MoS2 is composed of three stacked atomic layers (S–Mo–S) held together by van der Waals forces [49]. Eda et al. [50] reported on the structural and electronic properties of chemically exfoliated MoS2, finding that above the annealing temperature of 300 °C, semiconducting properties of the chemically exfoliated MoS2 are largely restored. Jin et al. [51] used the metallic chemically exfoliated 1T-MoS2 nanosheets via lithium intercalation from semiconducting 2H-MoS2 nanostructures grown directly on graphite to enhance the HER performance. Bulk MoS2 is a semiconductor with an indirect band gap of 1.2 eV, whereas single-layer MoS2 is a direct gap semiconductor with a band gap of 1.8 eV [52]. The results show that MoS2 exhibited better catalytic performance in various catalytic experiments when combined with other semiconductors. Li et al. [53] successfully synthesized the sphere-like MoS2/BiOBr composites via ethylene glycol (EG)-assisted solvothermal method. Due to the MoS2 loading, the MoS2/BiOBr composites exhibited much higher visible light photocatalytic activities towards rhodamine B (RhB) photodegradation than the pure BiOBr, with the highest photocatalysis efficiency obtained for 3 wt% MoS2/BiOBr composites.

Herein, we reported a novel composite photocatalyst MoS2/PbBiO2I prepared by a facile ILs-assisted solvothermal method. In our previous work, ionic liquids were employed to control the synthesis of Bi4O5Br2 ultrathin nanosheets [54] and BiOBr nanosquares [55] by simultaneously serving as reactants and templates. In this work, ionic liquid 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Hmim]I) is shown to not only act as a source and template but also as a dispersant. The morphologies and structures were analysed by XRD, SEM and TEM. The photocatalytic activity was determined by degradation of rhodamine B (RhB), antibiotic agent ciprofloxacin (CIP) and bisphenol A (BPA) under visible light. Several characterization techniques were used to analyse the optical and photoelectrical properties in detail. The main active species were determined via electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis and free radicals trapping experiments. The mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants was proposed based on the results obtained with these techniques.

All reagents used in this paper were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. MoS2 was synthesized according to the reports in the literature [56]. Typically, 1 mmol of molybdenum trioxide (MoO3) and 2.5 mmol of potassium thiocyanate (KSCN) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. This mixed solution was magnetically stirred at room temperature for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. Finally, the mixed solution was transferred into a 25 mL autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. The product was collected by centrifugation, washed several times with ethanol and deionized water, and dried at 50 °C to obtain the MoS2 sample. In a typical synthesis of MoS2/PbBiO2I composites, 0.5 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.5 mmol Pb(NO3)2 were dissolved in 15 mL of ethylene glycol with a certain amount of MoS2. The resulting mixture was first treated by ultrasonication for 1 h and then magnetically stirred for 30 min to obtain the homogeneous suspension A. Then, 0.5 mmol of 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Hmim]I) was dissolved in 5 mL of ethylene glycol to form solution B. Solution B was added dropwise to suspension A. After stirring for 30 min, the resultant suspension was poured into a 25 mL autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 24 h. The obtained reaction product was centrifuged, washed three times each with alcohol and deionized water, and then dried at 50 °C to obtain the MoS2/PbBiO2I composites. The composites with different MoS2 contents were labelled 0.5 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I, 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I, 1.5 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I, 3 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I, 5 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I, 7.5 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I and 15 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I, respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Shimadzu XRD-6000) patterns were recorded to analyse the crystalline structure of the as-prepared samples by Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 Å) at the scan rate of 7°/min. The elemental composition and the chemical states on the surface of pure PbBiO2I and MoS2/PbBiO2I materials were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a PHI5300 instrument with Mg-Kα radiation. The binding energy results were corrected for specimen charging by referencing the C 1s line to 284.6 eV in the analysis. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JEOL, JSM-7001F) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, JEM-2100) were used to analyse the morphology and microstructure of the samples. Diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were detected by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2450) with BaSO4 as the reference. The surface areas and particle size of the samples were obtained by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method based on the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms obtained using a Micromeritics TriStar II 3020 system. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were collected using a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer. The photocurrent and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were detected by a standard three electrode system of an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660B) with a 500 W Xe arc lamp as the photosource. All photoelectrochemical experiments were performed at ambient temperature using a conventional three-compartment cell with a platinum (Pt) wire as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode; MoS2/PbBiO2I sample films coated on ITO glasses with the area (0.5 × 1 cm2) were used as the working electrode. Aqueous phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7) was used in the photocurrent detection, and EIS was performed in a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4−.

The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared MoS2/PbBiO2I samples was evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB), antibiotic agent ciprofloxacin (CIP) and bisphenol A (BPA) with the initial concentration of 10 mg L−1. To prevent thermal catalysis in the experiment, the entire experimental procedure was carried out in a circulating water bath at 30 °C. First, 0.03 g of the MoS2/PbBiO2I composite was dissolved in 100 mL RhB, CIP or BPA solution. The light source is a 300 W xenon lamp (the lamp for degradation BPA is a 250 W xenon lamp) with a cut-off filter for λ > 400 nm. During the reaction, air is continuously fed to ensure sufficient oxygen and the catalyst and contaminant solution are thoroughly mixed with a magnetic stirrer. Prior to the irradiation, the suspensions were magnetically stirred for 30 min to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the catalyst and the contaminant. Then, 3 mL of the suspension was withdrawn from the reaction vessel at every 30 min (or 1 h). The catalyst particles were removed by centrifugation and the remaining RhB (or CIP) content in the solution was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 553 nm and 276 nm, respectively. The amount of remaining BPA was determined by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). BPA was detected by two Varian ProStar 210 pumps, an Agilent TC-C18 column, and a Varian ProStar 325 UV–vis Detector at 230 nm, in which the solution of methanol and H2O with the volume ratio 75: 25 was used as the mobile phase at 1 mL min−1, and 20 μL of the sample solution was injected.

The composition and crystal structure of MoS2/PbBiO2I composites were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. The XRD patterns of the as-prepared MoS2/PbBiO2I composites consisting of PbBiO2I with different MoS2 contents are shown in Fig. 1 . The diffraction peaks of MoS2/PbBiO2I composites at 29.6°, 31.2°, 44.7°, 51.5° and 54.5° corresponded to (1 0 3), (1 1 0), (2 0 0), (1 1 6) and (2 1 3) crystal facets, respectively, which can be indexed to the tetragonal phase of PbBiO2I (JCPDS card No. 38-1007) with the lattice parameters a = 4.053 Å, b = 4.053 Å, and c = 13.520 Å. The narrow and sharp peaks in the figure revealed that the PbBiO2I sample had good crystallinity, and comparison with the pure PbBiO2I material shows that the added MoS2 did not change the formation and crystal structure of the PbBiO2I material. The diffraction peaks of MoS2 were not found in the pattern, which may be related to the low content and good dispersion of MoS2 in MoS2/PbBiO2I and the low crystallinity of MoS2.

The chemical states and the surface element composition of the as-prepared MoS2/PbBiO2I composite, as well as pure PbBiO2I, were analysed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 a, Pb, Bi, O, I, C, Mo and S elements can be observed in the survey scan spectrum of 5 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I, corresponding to the chemical composition of MoS2/PbBiO2I. Pb, Bi, O, I and C elements and their valence states can be clearly obtained from the corresponding XPS (Fig. S1) data of the pure PbBiO2I sample. We note that the carbon element came from the carbon material sprayed on the surface during the sample testing. To further determine the valence states of the various elements in the MoS2/PbBiO2I composite, and especially the valence states of Mo and S, the results of a high-resolution XPS analysis of 5 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I samples are shown in Fig. 2(b–g). The photoelectron spectra of Pb 4f consisted of two peaks (Fig. 2b) with the binding energies of 138.0 eV and 142.8 eV, corresponding to Pb 4f7/2 and Pb 4f5/2 levels, respectively, indicating that the Pb in the composite photocatalyst was present in the Pb2+ state [57]. The peaks of the Bi 4f orbital appeared at 158.9 eV and 164.2 eV (Fig. 2c), indicating that Bi present in the composite material was in the form of Bi3+ [58]. The peak at 529.9 eV shown in Fig. 2d belongs to the O2− in the composite [59]. It can be seen from Fig. 2e that the I 3d consisted of two peaks at 618.9 eV and 630.5 eV, belonging to the I 3d3/2 and I 3d5/2 levels, respectively. Mo 3d at 232.1 eV and 235.2 eV were attributed to the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 orbitals [60], indicating that Mo present in MoS2/PbBiO2I composites was in the form of Mo4+. Although the two peaks of S 2p overlap with the peaks of Bi 4f, the presence of Mo still indicated the formation of MoS2, as also reported in a similar study in the literature [60]. XPS analysis showed that MoS2 was successfully compounded into PbBiO2I, and the synthesized material was MoS2/PbBiO2I composite photocatalyst. The results of XPS were in agreement with the XRD results, further suggesting that the as-obtained sample was a MoS2/PbBiO2I composite.

The morphologies of the as-prepared pure PbBiO2I, MoS2 and 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composites were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As seen from Fig. 3 a, the prepared PbBiO2I materials are spherical structures with the diameter of approximately 1.5 μm and the microspheres were assembled by nanosheets which were nearly 40–50 nm thick and with lateral dimensions of 200–300 nm. The MoS2 materials are nano-sized flaky structures with the size of approximately 100 nm as seen from Fig. 3b. The as-prepared 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composites are spherical structures assembled by nanosheets that were the same as the pure PbBiO2I composites; these have much higher surface roughness and much tighter inner structure compared with pure PbBiO2I. The same results were presented by the detection of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image which presented in Fig. S2. From the TEM images, it can be seen that there are many small nanosheets wrapped around the nanospheres in 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composites compared to the pure PbBiO2I. In addition, the diameters of the 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composites spherical structures are slightly smaller than that of the pure PbBiO2I samples. Due to the rougher surface, the specific surface area increases, thereby increasing the number of reactive sites and enhancing the photocatalytic performance. To explore the relationship between the morphologies and the amount of MoS2, the SEM images for different loading amounts (7.5 wt%, 15 wt%) are provided in Fig. S4, and it can be seen that the sphere-like structure of MoS2/PbBiO2I composite was somewhat broken when the MoS2 content was much increased and the degree of breaking was increased with increasing amount of MoS2. Therefore, it is important to select a proper amount of introduced MoS2 to achieve the best activity for degradation and better morphology. Elemental mapping (Fig. S3) unambiguously showed that 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composite consists of Bi, I, O, Pb, Mo and S elements uniformly distributed in the MoS2/PbBiO2I sample. The chemical composition of the MoS2/PbBiO2I microspheres was further confirmed by EDS analysis. Fig. 3d suggests that the MoS2/PbBiO2I microspheres are composed of mainly Pb, Bi, O, I, Mo and S. The EDS analysis further proves that the microspheres are pure MoS2/PbBiO2I composite.

As is well-known, the larger specific surface area of a photocatalyst could provide more surface active sites and enables absorption of a greater amount of pollutant on the photocatalyst, favouring the improvement of the photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the sample was further analysed using nitrogen adsorption-desorption. The BET specific surface area of the 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composite is approximately 39.02 m2/g, which is approximately 2.3 times greater than that of the pure PbBiO2I. A significant desorption line when P/P0 is between 0.8 and 1.0 can be seen from the nitrogen adsorption-desorption curve of the as-prepared sample (Fig. 4 a), suggesting that the sample is relatively holey. The pore size distribution of 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composite was calculated by the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method, and Fig. 4b shows that the pore diameter of the sample is approximately 20 nm and that it still contains a few microporous structures. It is concluded that the 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I material has a larger specific surface area and a smaller pore size than the pure PbBiO2I material. This plays an important role in the adsorption and separation, as well as in the catalytic reaction, for mesoporous materials because of the large specific surface area, regular and orderly pore structure, concentrated pore size distribution and continuously adjustable size of the MoS2/PbBiO2I sample. Thus, the MoS2/PbBiO2I material could be regarded as a good shape-selective catalyst. At the same time, the larger specific surface area can increase the diffusion of pollutants and can provide more reactive sites that could capture more active species and reactants on the material surface to significantly improve the photocatalytic performance of the material [61–63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and electronic properties of MoS2/PbBiO2I photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The photoresponse of the MoS2/PbBiO2I composites was determined by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). Fig. 5 shows the DRS spectra of the pure PbBiO2I material and the MoS2/PbBiO2I composite. It can be seen from the figure that the MoS2/PbBiO2I composite with different MoS2 contents showed effective light absorption for wavelengths less than 530 nm, demonstrating that the MoS2/PbBiO2I composites are effective visible-light-responsive photocatalysts. Moreover, the absorption intensity of the MoS2/PbBiO2I composites in the entire spectrum is obviously enhanced and the absorption edge of the MoS2/PbBiO2I composites is significantly redshifted compared to the pure PbBiO2I. Pure PbBiO2I are particles are dark yellow, and with the introduction of MoS2, the MoS2/PbBiO2I composites gradually become yellowish green, thereby showing enhanced light absorption. The inset of Fig. 5 demonstrated the conventional Tauc plot of crystalline PbBiO2I; the bandgap (1.98 eV) of PbBiO2I could be easily obtained by extrapolating to the x-axis. The introduction of MoS2 enhances the ability of PbBiO2I to respond to more visible light to produce more photogenerated carriers and promote its photocatalytic performance. This may be caused by the introduction of MoS2 increasing the specific surface area of the PbBiO2I material and thus enhancing the visible light absorption.

The transfer, migration, as well as separation efficiency, of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs are the key parameters in determining the photocatalytic performance of semiconductor catalysts [64]. In the absence of light, the photocurrent intensity is almost negligible; when the light source is turned on, the photocurrent in the material increases rapidly and gradually saturates at a certain value (Fig. 6 ). It is obvious that the transient photocurrent of 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I is almost 1.7 times higher than that of PbBiO2I, showing that 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I material has a higher separation rate of electrons and holes, and it can be seen that 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I material will display better photocatalytic organic pollutant degradation performance. To obtain more information regarding the mechanism, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were applied and were used to investigate the electron transfer resistance. Examination of Fig. S5 shows that the arc radius in the electrochemical impedance spectrum of the MoS2/PbBiO2I composites is smaller than that of pure PbBiO2I, which means that the MoS2/PbBiO2I composite shows a lower resistance than the pure PbBiO2I, indicating that the introduction of MoS2 into PbBiO2I could induce faster transfer of interfacial charges to enhance the photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalytic performance of the MoS2/PbBiO2I composites with different MoS2 contents was measured by degradation of RhB, CIP and BPA under visible light irradiation. Fig. 7 shows the activity of MoS2/PbBiO2I for the degradation of RhB by the pure PbBiO2I material and the different MoS2-loaded PbBiO2I composites. As seen from Fig. 7a, self-degradation of RhB is almost negligible, and when a certain amount of catalyst was added, the degradation of RhB was significantly accelerated, and the photocatalytic activity was higher for all MoS2/PbBiO2I composites compared to that of pure PbBiO2I. Specifically, the degradation efficiency of RhB with pure PbBiO2I was 60%, whereas 99% of RhB could be removed by 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I. These results indicated that the MoS2 modification significantly improved the photocatalytic performance of pure PbBiO2I materials. Notably, the composite photocatalyst displayed the highest photocatalytic activity when the content of MoS2 is 1.0 wt%. The photocatalytic performance of MoS2/PbBiO2I was gradually reduced when the added amount of MoS2 was greater than 1.0 wt%. Considering the black sheet structure of MoS2, a high amount of MoS2 attached to the surface of PbBiO2I microspheres affects the light absorption of PbBiO2I and then reduces its photocatalytic performance. Additionally, when the amount of MoS2 was increased to a certain value, the three-dimensional structure of PbBiO2I microspheres could no longer be formed because of the inhibition of assembly of PbBiO2I microspheres, which reduced the specific surface area and therefore the photocatalytic activity of the composites.

We also use colourless antibiotics ciprofloxacin (CIP) as a degradation substrate to eliminate the effects of dye sensitization. Currently, as the third-generation quinolone antibacterial drug, ciprofloxacin is usually used in the treatment of bacterial infections due to its wide antibiogram and excellent bactericidal effect. However, due to the human body’s inability to metabolise antibiotics and the frequent occurrence of drug reactions, ciprofloxacin may seriously endanger human health [65]. Furthermore, antibiotics are considered to be a new kind of environmental pollutants because of their negative effects on ecosystems [66,67]. Therefore, the rapid and efficient degradation of CIP has become particularly urgent, and exploration of effective techniques for CIP removal is important and imperative. Fig. 7b shows the gradual change of the absorption spectra of CIP degraded by 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composite. As shown in the figure, the absorbance of the maximum absorption peak of CIP at λmax = 276 nm decreases with the addition of 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I, indicating that CIP is gradually degraded. Fig. 7c shows the degradation activity of CIP for PbBiO2I and 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composite. It can be seen that CIP is very stable without the introduction of a catalyst. After 6 h, the degradation results of PbBiO2I and 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composites for CIP were 46% and 84%, respectively, indicating that the introduction of MoS2 favoured the improvement of the PbBiO2I photocatalytic activity.

To explore additional practical applications for pollutant photodegradation, the photocatalytic activity of MoS2/PbBiO2I composites are further evaluated using other types of pollutants, such as the endocrine-disrupting chemical BPA. Examination of Fig. 7d clearly shows that the degradation activities of 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composite and PbBiO2I photocatalysts are 92% and 64%, respectively, in 2 h. These results indicated that the introduction of MoS2 favoured the improvement of the PbBiO2I photocatalytic activity. It can also be deduced that the PbBiO2I microspheres modified by MoS2 nanosheets could degrade more than two different types of pollutants.

To evaluate the reusability and stability of the MoS2/PbBiO2I composite, recycling reactions were performed by degrading RhB with 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I material under visible light irradiation. Fig. S7 shows that no apparent deactivation of the photocatalysts is observed after five successive cycles. These results imply the high stability and absence of photocorrosion of the MoS2/PbBiO2I microsphere.

To elucidate the active species in the photocatalytic process, the electron spin resonance (ESR) of 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composites with 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline (DMPO) as a scavenger in a methanol solution and an aqueous solution were performed. The four characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 − (Fig. 8 a) were observed in the methanol dispersion of 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I material under visible light irradiation, and the signal intensity was significantly higher than that of pure PbBiO2I (Fig. 9 a). In addition, no DMPO-
OH signal peaks were detected in the aqueous solution of 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I material under dark conditions and visible light irradiation (Fig. 8b). ESR results showed that O2 is produced on the surface of the electron reducing material on the MoS2/PbBiO2I composite material during the photocatalysis process, and the
O2 − generation is enhanced by the introduction of MoS2 during the photocatalytic process, enhancing the photocatalytic performance. To further determine the main active species during the photodegradation, different free radicals trapping experiments of 1 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I composite with visible light irradiation have been carried out. This can be seen in Fig. S8, where the tertiary butanol, triethanolamine and nitrogen are used as the trapping agents for hydroxyl radicals (
OH), holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (
O2 −), respectively. The photodegradation efficiency was obviously decreased when the triethanolamine was added or when nitrogen was passed, while there was little decrease with the inclusion of tertiary butanol during the photocatalytic degradation reaction. Therefore, it can be ascertained that for the MoS2/PbBiO2I composite, the
OH did not play a major role while h+ and
O2 − were the main active species during the photodegradation under visible light irradiation. This result is in agreement with the results of ESR analysis.

Based on the above experimental results, the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of MoS2/PbBiO2I composites was proposed. The XPS valence band spectrum of PbBiO2I is presented in order to better determine the valence band (VB) as well as the conduction band (CB) of the PbBiO2I material. As shown in Fig. S6, the valence band is located at 0.83 eV. According to the DRS analysis and the formula Eg = EVB − ECB, the conduction band minimum (CBM) of PbBiO2I is −1.15 eV. The valence band, as well as the conduction band of MoS2, could be obtained from the literature [53]. As shown in Fig. 10 , when visible light is irradiated on the MoS2/PbBiO2I composite, PbBiO2I and MoS2 electrons are excited to the conduction band and leave holes in the valence band. It is found that the MoS2/PbBiO2I composite can absorb more visible light and generates more electron-hole pairs than the pure PbBiO2I. Due to the suitable energy band alignment (type II heterostructure) of MoS2 and PbBiO2I, the holes in the MoS2 valence band could transfer to the PbBiO2I, and the electrons will transfer from the conduction band of PbBiO2I to the conduction band of MoS2, thereby improving the separation efficiency of electrons and holes, which is consistent with the photocurrent results. The electrons in the MoS2 conduction band can react with the O2 adsorbed on the surface of the material to produce
O2 −, and,
O2 − as well as h+ can further decompose the contaminants adsorbed on the surface of the material into small molecules to achieve the degradation. Therefore, the introduction of MoS2 has a clearly essential effect for the photocatalytic performance of PbBiO2I.

On the basis of the previous study, PbBiO2X materials were synthesized via ionic liquid assisted solvothermal method [68,69]. During this process, the ionic liquid not only acted as reactant but could also be used as a high-performance template to improve the control of the system and thus facilitate the structural regulation of functional materials. To further improve the photocatalytic performance, few-layer MoS2 materials were employed as the modifier. Here, the ionic liquid ([Hmim]I) could further act as the dispersant to better disperse the MoS2 and give rise to a powerful interaction between MoS2 and the PbBiO2I material. The obtained MoS2/PbBiO2I composite photocatalysts presented a microsphere-like structure composed of PbBiO2I nanosheets, and their surfaces are attached to a large amount of MoS2. Because of the interface interaction between MoS2 and PbBiO2I, the MoS2/PbBiO2I composites presented larger specific surface area, enhanced light absorption region, and stronger photocurrent intensity compared to pure PbBiO2I, which means the presence of more active sites and more effective electron-hole pair generation. Thus, the photocatalytic activity of MoS2/PbBiO2I was higher than that of pure PbBiO2I, and 1.0 wt% MoS2/PbBiO2I showed the best photocatalytic activity. The main active species towards pollutant removal were determined to be
O2 − and h+.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21476098, 21576123 and 21676128) and the Science and Technology Support Program of Zhenjiang (SH2014018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.01.096.
